should_trigger: Design a scheduler for an agent runtime with 20 workers, scarce browser sessions, API rate limits, task priorities, and deadlines.
Our orchestrator keeps missing SLAs even though workers are nearly 100% utilized. Diagnose the scheduling policy and propose a better dispatching rule.
We have research jobs, test jobs, code-edit jobs, and deployment jobs competing for specialized tools. Decide which tasks should run first and when to reserve capacity.
Build a queue policy that prevents high-priority customer incidents from starving long-running maintenance jobs.
Should our auto-orchestrator preempt running tasks when an urgent job arrives? Model the interruption cost, deadlines, and rescheduling trigger.
should_not_trigger: Summarize Michael Pinedo's career and list his books.
Draw a BPMN diagram with an exclusive choice, parallel split, and synchronizing merge.
Break this vague product goal into a hierarchy of subtasks without assigning resources or execution times.
edge_cases: We only have a FIFO queue and two identical workers. Decide whether scheduling theory adds value or whether FIFO is sufficient.
A workflow has both branching joins and scarce GPU allocation. Decide which parts need workflow-control analysis and which parts need scheduling analysis.
The runtime can only expose custom worker code, not a central scheduler. Determine how to make the hidden scheduling policy observable and testable.
